top5Opportunities.statement: Minimize the likelihood of door guardians that pass commissioning tests but fail to detect activations under real operating conditions
Minimize unauthorized emergency exit activations going undetected between scheduled FM inspection visits
Minimize emergency exit event log gaps that cause an FM client to fail an unannounced fire safety inspection
Minimize conduit wiring and electrical installation cost per door in FM buildings not pre-wired for alarm systems
Minimize FM labor-hours required to compile audit-ready fire safety inspection certificates per client building

needs.statement: Minimize installation labor-hours per door when deploying exit monitoring in occupied FM client buildings
needs.importanceRationale: FM operators deploy across multiple client sites simultaneously; per-door labor hours directly scale total deployment cost and timeline.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Wireless systems enable self-install in under 30 minutes per door. Wired panels (45%) require electricians adding 2-4 hours per door. Market-weighted satisfaction is moderate.
needs.primaryStakeholder: FM safety compliance engineer / compliance technician
stakeholderIds: FM safety compliance engineer / compliance technician

needs.statement: Minimize conduit wiring and electrical installation cost per door in FM buildings not pre-wired for alarm systems
needs.importanceRationale: Most FM-managed buildings are not pre-wired for alarm systems; conduit wiring cost per door is the primary barrier to comprehensive exit monitoring deployment.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Wired alarm panels (45% share) require full conduit and electrical installation per door. This is the dominant incumbent and its cost structure drives low market satisfaction.
needs.primaryStakeholder: FM safety compliance engineer / compliance technician
stakeholderIds: FM safety compliance engineer / compliance technician

needs.statement: Minimize battery depletion events reaching critical failure before a CMMS maintenance alert is generated for the affected door guardian
needs.importanceRationale: Battery depletion below monitoring threshold creates a silent gap in FM coverage; client buildings may be unprotected between scheduled maintenance visits.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No incumbent technology provides proactive CMMS-integrated battery health alerts. Mechanical alarms have no battery. Wired panels are mains-powered. Wireless systems lack CMMS integration.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of door guardians that pass commissioning tests but fail to detect activations under real operating conditions
needs.importanceRationale: A guardian that passes commissioning but fails in operation creates a false sense of security; the FM operator believes the door is protected when it is not.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Commissioning tests are conducted under controlled conditions. No incumbent system validates ongoing detection capability post-commissioning. Mechanical-only alarms (20%) and manual patrols (25%) have zero operational validation.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of an FM-managed emergency exit being found non-compliant with EN 179 or EN 1125 at a fire marshal inspection
needs.importanceRationale: EN 179/1125 non-compliance at fire marshal inspection creates liability for the FM operator and can result in client contract termination.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Compliance verification relies on manual inspection by FM technicians. No automated EN 179/1125 compliance monitoring exists. Discovery is inspection-based, not continuous.

needs.statement: Minimize elapsed time between unauthorized exit activation and FM operations center notification
needs.importanceRationale: FM operations centers manage multiple client buildings; rapid notification enables dispatch prioritization and reduces exposure window.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Wired panels (45%) alert a local panel but not the FM operations center directly. Mechanical alarms (20%) are local-only. Manual patrols (25%) have hours-to-detect latency. Wireless IoT provides push but has limited deployment.
needs.primaryStakeholder: FM safety compliance engineer / compliance technician
stakeholderIds: FM safety compliance engineer / compliance technician

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit alarm failure during a building-wide power loss event
needs.importanceRationale: Power loss events are the highest-risk scenarios for exit security; alarm failure during power loss eliminates the last line of defense.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Mechanical alarm incumbents (Alt C, 20%) are inherently power-independent. Battery-powered wireless systems maintain operation. Only wired panels (45%) are vulnerable but have UPS options. Satisfaction is relatively high at 7/10.
needs.primaryStakeholder: FM safety compliance engineer / compliance technician
stakeholderIds: FM safety compliance engineer / compliance technician

needs.statement: Minimize unauthorized emergency exit activations going undetected between scheduled FM inspection visits
needs.importanceRationale: FM inspection intervals are typically weekly or monthly; undetected activations between visits represent extended security exposure for client buildings.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Mechanical-only alarms (20%) and manual patrol rounds (25%) provide zero detection between inspection visits. Wired panels detect but lack FM portfolio integration. Satisfaction is very low at 2/10.
needs.primaryStakeholder: FM safety compliance engineer / compliance technician
stakeholderIds: FM safety compliance engineer / compliance technician

needs.statement: Minimize integration effort required to relay exit event data into the FM company's CMMS or CAFM system
needs.importanceRationale: FM companies operate on CMMS/CAFM platforms for work order management; exit monitoring data must flow into these systems for operational efficiency.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No exit monitoring system provides native CMMS/CAFM integration. Wired panels use proprietary protocols. Manual data entry from paper logs is the dominant practice.

needs.statement: Minimize the proportion of deployed door guardians with critically depleted batteries across the FM portfolio at any time
needs.importanceRationale: A high proportion of depleted batteries across the portfolio means multiple client buildings have reduced or no monitoring simultaneously.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No portfolio-level battery health dashboard exists. Mechanical alarms have no batteries. Wired panels are mains-powered. Battery monitoring across distributed deployments is a fundamental gap.

needs.statement: Minimize the time to identify exact door location and dispatch a technician following an alarm event
needs.importanceRationale: Door-level location identification enables targeted dispatch rather than building-wide search by the responding technician.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Wired panels identify zones but not individual doors. Wireless systems can identify specific devices. Manual patrol requires physical search. Satisfaction is moderate.

needs.statement: Minimize the risk of FM client insurance premium increases due to gaps in documented exit monitoring coverage
needs.importanceRationale: Insurers increasingly require documented exit monitoring; coverage gaps can trigger premium surcharges or coverage exclusions for FM clients.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Documentation quality varies widely. Wired panels provide basic logging. Mechanical alarms and patrols produce no automated records. Coverage documentation is mostly manual.

needs.statement: Minimize FM labor-hours required to compile audit-ready fire safety inspection certificates per client building
needs.importanceRationale: FM operators manage dozens of client buildings; manual compilation of audit-ready certificates per building is a major labor cost multiplier.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No automated compliance certificate generation exists. Manual compilation from paper logs and disparate systems is universal. Satisfaction is very low at 2/10.

needs.statement: Minimize emergency exit event log gaps that cause an FM client to fail an unannounced fire safety inspection
needs.importanceRationale: Unannounced fire safety inspections require complete event logs; gaps expose the FM operator to client liability claims and contract termination.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Mechanical alarms (20%) and manual patrols (25%) produce zero digital event logs. Wired panels log zone events but coverage is incomplete. Log gaps are endemic across the market.

needs.statement: Minimize unavailability of real-time portfolio-level exit status data for FM client account management reporting
needs.importanceRationale: FM account managers need portfolio-level status visibility to demonstrate service quality and proactively manage client relationships.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No incumbent technology provides portfolio-level exit status aggregation. Each building is a separate data silo. Real-time portfolio reporting does not exist.

needs.statement: Minimize unplanned FM site visits caused by alarm activations that battery monitoring data could have predicted
needs.importanceRationale: Unplanned site visits are costly for FM operators; predictive battery monitoring would enable proactive maintenance scheduling.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Some unplanned visits are inherent to reactive alarm response. Battery monitoring is absent but the need is moderate — most activations are genuine events, not battery-related.

needs.statement: Minimize total lifecycle cost-per-door for exit monitoring hardware, installation, and maintenance across FM portfolios of 100+ doors
needs.importanceRationale: FM portfolios of 100+ doors magnify per-door cost; lifecycle cost including installation, maintenance, and replacement drives procurement decisions.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Wired panels (45%) have high installation cost. Mechanical alarms are cheap but offer no monitoring. Total lifecycle cost per door remains high across all incumbent approaches.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of authorized door guardian re-arming after activation creating a false event in the compliance log
needs.importanceRationale: False compliance log entries from authorized re-arming create noise in audit records but do not represent a significant safety risk.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Most systems adequately distinguish re-arming events from genuine activations. The need is a minor operational friction, not a fundamental gap.

needs.statement: Minimize wireless monitoring coverage gaps in large-footprint FM buildings caused by 868 MHz EasyWave signal attenuation through reinforced concrete and steel
needs.importanceRationale: FM-managed buildings include diverse construction types; reinforced concrete and steel structures attenuate 868 MHz signals, creating potential blind spots.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Repeater infrastructure mitigates but adds per-building cost. No automated signal monitoring exists to detect coverage degradation over time.

needs.statement: Minimize EasyWave radio module battery depletion below monitoring threshold between FM maintenance visits in high-traffic deployments exceeding 5 activations per day
needs.importanceRationale: High-traffic FM doors (>5 activations/day) accelerate battery drain; depletion between maintenance visits leaves the door unmonitored.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No published power consumption data for high-frequency activation scenarios. Field estimation is required per deployment, creating maintenance planning uncertainty.

needs.statement: Minimize integration rework required to relay EasyWave alarm events to client BMS systems on BACnet or KNX via GfS-proprietary BUS gateway intermediary
needs.importanceRationale: FM clients often have existing BACnet/KNX infrastructure; proprietary gateway integration creates per-building rework cost.
needs.satisfactionRationale: EasyWave-to-BACnet/KNX bridging requires proprietary gateway hardware per building. Integration effort is repeated across each client site in the FM portfolio.

needs.statement: Minimize FM contract rejections in premium buildings due to surface-mounted door guardian protrusion conflicting with interior design requirements
needs.importanceRationale: Premium building clients may reject surface-mounted devices for aesthetic reasons; this is a niche concern affecting a small subset of the FM portfolio.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Surface-mount profile is acceptable for most commercial buildings. Premium building rejections are rare and can be mitigated through alternative mounting approaches.
